title: Asynchronous component tests

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Asynchronous component tests; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Testing Library; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Asynchronous component tests
sources.claim: Async React tests trigger realistic actions and await observable state with find or wait utilities instead of fixed sleeps.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Async React tests trigger realistic actions and await observable state with find or wait utilities instead of fixed sleeps.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A test clicks Load and awaits the course heading after a mocked response while also covering an error state. Your goal is to test loading, success, empty, error, retry and stale-request behavior deterministically. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Accessibility, errors and performance; Routing and application context; Accessible queries and interactions; Client fetch lifecycle. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Async React tests trigger realistic actions and await observable state with find or wait utilities instead of fixed sleeps. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Fake timers need controlled flushing, unresolved promises leak, and waiting for implementation calls can miss user behavior. This draft therefore rejects: Adding a one-second timeout is the most reliable way to wait for React updates.

includes: Wait for observable state transitions and mock the network boundary rather than internal hooks.
Test loading, success, empty, error, retry and stale-request behavior deterministically.
excludes: Fake timers need controlled flushing, unresolved promises leak, and waiting for implementation calls can miss user behavior.

outcomes: Test loading, success, empty, error, retry and stale-request behavior deterministically.

examples.title: Asynchronous component tests: concrete case
examples.situation: A test clicks Load and awaits the course heading after a mocked response while also covering an error state.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A test clicks Load and awaits the course heading after a mocked response while also covering an error state.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Await the observable condition rather than elapsed time, and control promises or timers deterministically.
examples.result: The result follows because Await the observable condition rather than elapsed time, and control promises or timers deterministically.

analogy.example: A state-driven component workshop can model Asynchronous component tests, where inputs and state produce a UI description.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Asynchronous component tests before using exact platform terms
limitations: A workshop does not reproduce reconciliation identity, hook ordering, concurrent rendering, effects, or browser accessibility APIs.
